ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers cooked noodles as a category; no specific code exists for wonton noodle with peanut sesame sauce. Mapped to closest defensible parent node.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is approximately 400 g (one bowl), providing around 684 kcal, 76 g protein, 42 g fat, and an estimated 60–80 g carbohydrate based on typical noodle dish composition.

nutrient_source.title: 麻酱菜肉馄饨面 Noodle With Vegetable & Pork Wantons In Peanut Sesame Sauce — HPB Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for energy, protein, fat, sodium, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, and cholesterol are taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish (cr_id G00010061). Carbohydrate and sugar are recorded as 0.0 g/100g in the HPB source; however, this is nutritionally inconsistent with a wheat-noodle-based dish. The HPB values are preserved as reported per source policy, with a flag in notes. Saturated fat data not reported by HPB; estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous entry for wonton noodle soup (USDA SR Legacy #21340 and peanut sauce component) as an approximate value. Dietary fibre reported as 0.0 g/100g by HPB source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. GI of 52 is estimated based on the analogous food 'wheat noodles, boiled' (GI ~52, low category) from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). The peanut sesame sauce contributes fat and protein which further attenuate glycaemic response. Glycemic load per serving is set to 0.0 because the HPB source records carbohydrate_g as 0.0 g/100g; this is a known data anomaly documented in notes — actual GL for a 400g serving of this dish would be approximately 26–42 based on typical noodle carbohydrate content of 13–21g/100g cooked.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold; a single 400g serving delivers approximately 4200 mg sodium, nearly double the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, request sauce on the side and limit portion to no more than 150–200 g, pairing with potassium-rich vegetables to partially offset sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Wheat noodles as the primary staple contribute significant refined carbohydrates (estimated 13–21 g/100g cooked) with minimal dietary fibre; the HPB-recorded 0 g carbohydrate is a known data anomaly and does not reflect actual glycaemic impact.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request wholemeal or lower-GI noodle alternatives, limit portion size to reduce carbohydrate load, and pair with high-fibre vegetables to blunt post-meal glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 1050 mg/100g drives hypertensive cardiovascular risk; pork filling and peanut sesame sauce contribute saturated fat and cholesterol (89 mg/100g), elevating LDL-associated risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish due to its very high sodium content and moderate saturated fat; if consumed, choose a smaller portion, request reduced sauce, and balance the meal with unsaturated-fat-rich foods and high-fibre vegetables.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium at 1050 mg/100g exceeds RA threshold; pork contributes arachidonic acid (omega-6) which amplifies inflammatory cytokine pathways; refined wheat noodles increase glycaemic load and systemic inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting pork filling with tofu or chicken, choosing wholegrain noodle alternatives, and requesting sauce on the side to reduce sodium-driven inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Noodle with Vegetable and Pork Wontons in Peanut Sesame Sauce (麻酱菜肉馄饨面, má jiàng cài ròu húntún miàn) is a Chinese-origin noodle dish popular in Singapore, consisting of springy wheat-based noodles served with boiled wontons filled with minced pork and vegetables, tossed in a bold, savoury peanut sesame sauce. The sauce is typically made from sesame paste (zhī ma jiàng), peanut butter, soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, and chilli, delivering a rich, nutty, slightly spicy flavour profile with a smooth, coating texture. The noodles are chewy and elastic, while the wontons provide a tender, juicy bite. Per 100g, the dish provides 171 kcal, 19.1g protein, 10.5g fat, and 1050mg sodium as reported by the HPB Singapore Foodo database. The carbohydrate value is recorded as 0g in the HPB source, which is a known data anomaly — wheat noodles typically contribute 13–21g carbohydrate per 100g cooked, making this dish a moderate-to-high glycaemic load food in realistic portions. Sodium content is very high at 1050mg/100g, making this dish a concern for individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. The peanut sesame sauce is a major sodium and fat contributor. Clinically, this dish is not recommended for individuals with hypertension or Type 2 Diabetes without significant modification. It is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner meal in Singapore's Chinese food culture, available at hawker centres and Chinese noodle stalls.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, sodium, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database, which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. The carbohydrate anomaly (0g) is preserved as reported and flagged. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA analogous data and documented. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database analogous food (wheat noodles, boiled). Confidence is high for HPB-reported fields; medium for estimated fields.
notes: CARBOHYDRATE ANOMALY: carbohydrate_g and sugar_g are recorded as 0.0 g/100g per the HPB Singapore Foodo source. This is nutritionally inconsistent with a wheat-noodle-based dish; typical cooked wheat noodles contain 13–21g carbohydrate per 100g. The HPB value is preserved as reported per source policy. Downstream users should treat carbohydrate_g = 0.0 as a data anomaly and apply typical noodle carbohydrate values for clinical or dietary calculations. ZERO-MACRO JUSTIFICATION: carbohydrate_g = 0.0 and sugar_g = 0.0 are confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database source for this specific entry (cr_id G00010061); however, this is flagged as a likely data anomaly rather than a naturally zero value — the dish contains wheat noodles which inherently contribute carbohydrates. dietary_fibre_g = 0.0 is confirmed zero per HPB source; this may reflect the refined nature of the noodles and wonton wrappers, though some fibre from vegetable filling would be expected. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for G00010061, and no equivalent complete panel was found in USDA FoodData Central for this exact dish as of 2026-04-16. SATURATED FAT: saturated_fat_g = 2.4 is an approximate estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central analogous data for wonton noodle soup and peanut sauce components; this is not directly reported by HPB. GLYCEMIC LOAD NOTE: glycemic_load_per_serving is set to 0.0 because the source-reported carbohydrate_g is 0.0 (data anomaly); actual GL for a 400g serving would be approximately 26–42 based on realistic noodle carbohydrate content.
